content.problemStatement:
Given an integer NNN, let us consider a triangle of numbers of NNN lines in which a number a11a_{11}a11​ appears in the first line, two numbers a21a_{21}a21​ and a22a_{22}a22​ appear in the second line, three numbers a31a_{31}a31​, a32a_{32}a32​ and a33a_{33}a33​ appear in the third line, etc. In general, iii numbers ai1,ai2…aiia_{i1}, a_{i2} \dots a_{ii}ai1​,ai2​…aii​ appear in the ithi^{th}ith line for all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N. Develop a program that will compute the largest of the sums of numbers that appear on the paths starting from the top towards the base, so that:


on each path the next number is located on the row below, more precisely either directly below or below and one place to the right.
**Warning:** large Input/Output data, be careful with certain languages


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases.
Then T test cases follow. Each test case starts with an integer NNN, the number of rows. Then NNN lines follow where in ithi^{th}ith line contains iii integers ai1,ai2…aiia_{i1}, a_{i2} \dots a_{ii}ai1​,ai2​…aii​.


Output Format
For each test case print the maximum path sum in a separate line.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of rows of the triangle. The next N lines contain the triangle: the i‑th of these lines contains i space‑separated integers a_{i1}, a_{i2}, …, a_{ii}.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum possible sum of a path from the top of the triangle to its base, moving only to the directly‑below or below‑right element at each step.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: best path 1→2→2 gives sum 5. Second test case: best path 1→1→4→3 gives sum 9.
Only one element exists, so the maximum path sum equals that element.
Two possible paths: 5+1=6 and 5+9=14. The maximum is 14.

solution.approach: The optimal substructure is: the best sum starting from a cell (i,j) equals the value at that cell plus the maximum of the best sums of the two cells directly below it, (i+1,j) and (i+1,j+1). By processing the triangle from the bottom row upwards we can compute these values in O(N^2) time. The answer for a test case is the value computed for the top cell (1,1). This bottom‑up DP uses only the triangle itself (or a separate DP array) and fits easily within the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, O(total N^2) over all test cases
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) if a separate DP table is used; it can be reduced to O(N) by reusing a single row, but O(N^2) comfortably fits the constraints.
